text: Three-D augmented reality robotic assisted segmental ureterectomy with buccal mucosal graft for the repair of upper complex ureteral strictures. Ureteral stenosis constitutes a very complex surgical scenario with a high rate of postoperative failure. For two to three centimeters upper strictures, a robotic-assisted segmental ureteral resection with subsequent uretero-ureteral anastomosis is considered a valid treatment option.
The surgeon uses a buccal mucosa graft to construct the anterior plate of the uretero-ureteral anastomosis, aiming to reduce suture tension.

In order to intraoperatively locate the stenosis, a concomitant ureteroscopy is usually employed. However, the addition of the endoscopy increases the intraoperative complexity and costs of the surgery and may lead to iatrogenic damage to the ureter, increasing the risk of a post-surgical treatment failure.

Among the alternative strategies, ICG and the luminal injection has been proposed as an intraoperative aid to identify the stenosis borders, but it needs a nephrostomium site as well as the intraoperative ureteroscopy employment. Three-D virtual models have been also used as a guide to locate the stricture in a cognitive way intraoperatively.

In order to increase the precision in identifying the stricture during the surgical dissection of the ureter, a step further to the use of cognitive three-D guidance has to be done, testing the role of augmented reality surgical navigation. Such technology has already demonstrated great usefulness in identifying hidden targets in other surgical settings, such as prostate lesions, kidney neoplasms and iliac artery plaques in kidney transplant.

In case of upper ureteral strictures not detectable on the organ surface, as it happens for stones embedded into the ureteral wall, the augmented reality technology can allow the precise identification of the target, minimizing the removal of peristenotic healthy tissue during the surgical dissection.
An assistant uses a three-D mouse to manually superimpose a three-D virtual model onto the operative field, visible in real time through Tile Pro technology connected to the DaVinci Surgical Console.

The surgeon uses the lower pole of the kidney and the intersection with the iliac vessels as anatomical markers to achieve a precise overlay of the virtual ureter model onto the real ureter during the procedure.
In the following video, three cases of different level ureteral strictures located above the iliac vessels crossing are shown.

In the first case, the surgeon uses augmented reality to superimpose a virtual model of the ureter and embedded stone over the operative field. The stricture is precisely located, and the ureter is incised to resect the stenotic tract. Ureteroscopy is performed to confirm the accuracy of the virtual model alignment.

In the second case, the surgeon prepares an omental flap for reconstruction. Using augmented reality, the virtual model of the ureter and stone is superimposed onto the real anatomy. The stone-related stricture in the middle portion of the upper ureter is identified, and the ureter is incised and resected, preserving a significant amount of healthy peristenotic ureter. The embedded stone is visibly detectable during the resection.
In the third case, the surgeon identifies the stricture located just below the ureteropelvic junction, with visible microcalcifications on the ureteral wall.

Indocyanine green (ICG) is injected through the nephrostomy, but it does not provide clear feedback on the stricture level. The ureter is visibly cut and spatulated, marking the beginning of the reconstructive phase.
The surgeon prepares a buccal mucosal graft by contouring a three by one centimeter section of oral mucosa. The graft is then cut using forceps in preparation for the robotic phase of the surgery.
The surgeon performs the fetting of the buccal mucosal graft, placing it into the abdominal cavity after completing the posterior plate of the anastomosis with a terminal-terminal suture.

The surgeon applies the mucosal graft over the ureteral defect, suturing it to the peripheral free margins of the anastomized ureteral segment. The graft tissue is carefully modeled on the defect until complete repair is achieved, allowing the progression of surgical instruments through the newly calibrated ureter.
The surgeon places the omental flap around the repaired ureter to ensure vascularization of the graft, aiming to prevent stricture recurrence.
In all the three cases, the surgery was successful without significant intra- and post-operative complications. All the examinations performed confirmed the resolution of the striptease.

Augmented reality intraoperative guidance showed to be a useful and precise technological tool able to correctly identify the ureteral stricture even when not visible on the ureteral wall surface, leading to maximize the preservation of the ureteral peristrictural healthy tissue.
